topics.title: Logging — SLF4J, Log4j2 & Logback
topics.description: Logging is the primary observability mechanism in Java backends. SLF4J (Simple Logging Facade for Java) is the universal API — your code calls SLF4J, and the implementation (Logback, Log4j2, java.util.logging) is swapped via classpath. Log levels hierarchy: TRACE < DEBUG < INFO < WARN < ERROR. In production, INFO is standard; DEBUG is too verbose. Logback is Spring Boot's default (logback-spring.xml). Log4j2 is faster (async appenders, LMAX Disruptor). MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) attaches contextual key-value pairs (requestId, userId) to every log line from the current thread.
topics.story: Imagine you're an air traffic controller. During normal operations, you hear routine check-ins (INFO). When weather changes, you hear WARN. When an aircraft declares emergency, ERROR. You never broadcast every thought from every pilot (TRACE/DEBUG) — the radio would be useless. But when an incident happens, you need the black box recording (DEBUG logs saved to file but not displayed). SLF4J is your radio protocol — it works with any radio brand. Logback/Log4j2 are the radio hardware. MDC is the flight number printed on every transmission so you can trace all messages from flight UA123 across 100 simultaneous transmissions.
topics.analogy: SLF4J = USB standard — plug any device (Logback, Log4j2) into the same port (your code). Log levels = urgency stamps: TRACE=post-it note, DEBUG=internal memo, INFO=public announcement, WARN=caution sign, ERROR=fire alarm. Logger per class = return address on each letter — you know exactly which class sent each log. MDC = a sticky label on every envelope from a specific customer — filter all their envelopes at once. Appender = destination: ConsoleAppender=printer, FileAppender=filing cabinet, RollingFileAppender=auto-archiving cabinet, AsyncAppender=inbox tray (non-blocking). Pattern layout format: %d{ISO} [%thread] %-5level %logger{36} - %msg%n
topics.memoryAnchor: SLF4J: Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyClass.class). log.debug('val={}', val) — {} is placeholder, avoids string concat. Levels: TRACE < DEBUG < INFO < WARN < ERROR. Spring Boot default: Logback via logback-spring.xml. MDC: MDC.put('reqId', id) at filter entry; MDC.clear() in finally. Async logging: <asyncAppender> wraps sync appender; default queue 256, discards DEBUG/INFO under pressure. Log4j2 async: AsyncLogger with Disruptor — zero GC pressure. Log rotation: <rollingPolicy maxFileSize='10MB' maxHistory='30'/>. Never log passwords, tokens, PII. Structured logging: log.info('{"event":"login","user":"{}"}', username) or use Logstash encoder.
topics.firstPrinciples: String concatenation in log statements wastes CPU even when the log level is disabled — 'DEBUG is off' but 'Error: ' + heavyComputation() still runs. SLF4J's {} placeholders only format when the level is active. Synchronous logging blocks the application thread on I/O. Async appenders move I/O to a background thread, dramatically reducing logging impact on throughput. MDC uses a ThreadLocal internally — must clear() in finally or it leaks across thread-pool-reused threads. Logback's <springProfile> lets you configure per-environment without multiple files.
topics.bruteForce: System.out.println() — no level, no timestamp, no class context, no control, not thread-safe across buffers. String concatenation inside log calls: log.debug('User: ' + user.getName() + ' role: ' + role) — runs getName() and role even when DEBUG is disabled. Single global logger instead of per-class loggers — cannot filter by package. No MDC — impossible to trace one user's request across 50 log lines.
topics.optimizedApproach: SLF4J with {} placeholders everywhere. Per-class loggers (private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyClass.class)). MDC filter in servlet filter or Spring interceptor — put requestId at entry, clear at finally. RollingFileAppender with 10MB files, 30-day retention. AsyncAppender for production (discardingThreshold=0 to never discard). Separate error log appender (level=ERROR only) for alerting. Structured JSON logging (Logstash encoder) for log aggregation (ELK/Splunk). Log at the right level: don't WARN on expected user errors, don't INFO on hot paths.
topics.whenToUse: INFO: business-significant events (user logged in, order placed, payment processed). DEBUG: request/response details, SQL queries in dev. WARN: recoverable issues, deprecated API usage, missing optional config. ERROR: unhandled exceptions, DB failures, external service down. TRACE: extremely fine-grained (frame-by-frame protocol debugging). Never log in tight loops — aggregate or sample.

examples.title: SLF4J Basics — Levels, Placeholders, MDC
examples.explanation: Three rules to memorize: (1) always use {} placeholders — never string concatenation (avoids formatting cost when level disabled), (2) always pass the exception as the final argument to log.error() — not e.getMessage(), because that loses the stack trace, (3) always MDC.clear() in a finally block — thread-pool threads are reused, and leftover MDC from request A will corrupt request B's logs.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Boot's RequestLoggingFilter or a custom OncePerRequestFilter is the standard place to put MDC.put('requestId', UUID.randomUUID()) at the start of every request. Correlation IDs from MDC flow into ELK/Splunk searches: find all logs with requestId=abc123 across Auth, OrderService, PaymentService microservices. Spring Sleuth/Micrometer Tracing auto-populates MDC with trace ID and span ID for distributed tracing.

examples.title: Logback Configuration — Per-Environment, Async, Rolling
examples.explanation: The logback-spring.xml uses <springProfile> to avoid separate files per environment — one file handles all profiles. The AsyncAppender wraps any other appender to make logging non-blocking: the application thread enqueues the log event and continues; a background thread does the I/O. Setting discardingThreshold=0 is important for production — default drops TRACE/DEBUG when the queue is 80% full, which can hide important debug logs during incident investigation. The additivity=false flag is often forgotten — without it, the same log line appears twice (from the package logger AND root).
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Boot auto-configures Logback — just provide logback-spring.xml. The logstash-logback-encoder library converts log output to JSON: {"@timestamp":"...","level":"INFO","logger":"...","message":"...","requestId":"..."}. This feeds directly into Elasticsearch/Kibana for searchable log aggregation. Sentry/Datadog Logback appenders can send ERROR-level logs directly to alerting platforms without separate configuration.

problems.title: Fix the Logging Anti-Patterns
problems.description: The following code has 5 logging bugs: (1) string concatenation in debug, (2) exception logged without stack trace, (3) missing MDC.clear(), (4) wrong log level for expected business event, (5) logging in a tight loop. Identify and fix each.
problems.hint1: Use {} placeholders instead of + concatenation. Pass exception as last arg to get stack trace.
problems.hint2: MDC.put() without MDC.clear() in finally leaks context across thread-pool threads.
problems.hint3: Logging inside a loop of n=10000 can generate 10000 log entries — aggregate before or after.
problems.testCases:
Bug 1: use {} not +
Bug 2: log.error(msg, e) not log.error(e.getMessage())
Bug 3: MDC.clear() in finally
Bug 4: INFO not WARN for logout
Bug 5: aggregate log, not per-item

problems.title: Request Correlation with MDC Filter
problems.description: Write a servlet Filter (or Spring OncePerRequestFilter) that: (1) generates a UUID requestId at the start of each request, (2) reads X-Request-ID header if present (use caller's ID for distributed tracing), (3) stores it in MDC as 'requestId', (4) also stores the authenticated user (from session, or 'anonymous'), (5) always clears MDC in finally. Then write a service method that logs using the MDC context.
problems.hint1: Extract header: request.getHeader('X-Request-ID'), fallback to UUID.randomUUID().toString().
problems.hint2: Session attribute for user: HttpSession session = request.getSession(false); if session != null, getAttribute('user').
problems.hint3: MDC.clear() must be in the finally block after chain.doFilter() — not after, because the chain may throw.
problems.testCases:
Filter reads X-Request-ID or generates UUID
Filter puts requestId + user in MDC
All service log lines include MDC values
MDC.clear() in finally block
